sentences.text: They put us into trains.
After they give us the clothes and everything else ready, they give us some food for the train.
And I don't remember how long it took us, the train, but we got into Warsaw.
Oh, yeah.
Cattle car, again, but not as many as-- what do you call?
They didn't put us like sardines.
We had enough room on the train, because I don't remember any-- having any hard time in the train.
Because it must have been a very, very short distance.
But I don't remember.
I don't recall exactly how long it took us from Auschwitz to Warsaw.
So we got into Warsaw, and they took us into this particular camp, where they start-- which actually was not even complete lager.
So we had nothing there, actually.
We started building themselves, different barracks.
And we were only about a few hundred, only Greek Jews.
Only Greek Jews who were there at the time.
Because the only reason to Greek Jews is because we couldn't speak the language.
And we could easily escaped, you know.
Because there was no-- and the only reason they took us over there is to clean up the mess right after the Polish-- the Jews in Warschau, the Germans bombed them out.
What do you call this special word for it?
Was it the uprising?
The uprising, yeah.
After the uprising.
And then the ghetto was completely empty.
No.
Never.
No, we never had any news whatsoever.
No news.
Really, [INAUDIBLE].
No radios, no newspaper, nothing.
We didn't know about it.
We didn't even know where Warsaw was.
Especially, we Greeks, we didn't knew nothing about it.
So they started, they organized a kitchen.
And they start a few blocks, barracks for us.
It was two in a bed.
Like triple decker, two in each-- like Auschwitz, we had.
And it wasn't that bad, but we didn't have much.
We didn't have anything.
We didn't have no hospital.
We had no-- absolutely nothing.
But the people who went to work there, they asked who is-- what do you call it-- bakers?
So I figured, I'm a baker, you know.
Everybody was a baker, too.
Instead of put them into a baker, they picked me to pick up potatoes.
Go and pick up, in the cold weather, potatoes.
So that was my baker job.
But out of the rest of the guys, they were working into the Warschau city.
I mean, Warschau ghetto, cleaning up.
It was all the buildings were all standing up a half.
And they were cleaning up, or-- they were finding a lot of gold pots with shit on top and gold underneath.
Like they found-- a lot of the stuff they found over there is Greek Jews.
Not me, because I never worked in this particular commando.
And slowly, they started getting organized in the Warsaw ghetto.
It wasn't that bad.
The only trouble was if you get sick, you're dead.
You see, there was no hospital.
You get sick, if a pill will not do the job-- if the doctor sees that you--
So what they do is get undressed.
You go to see the doctor.
He gives you a needle.
By the time you go and put your clothes on, you're dead.
You die.
You know?
They kill you.
That was the only bad thing that we had in Warschau ghetto.
And then we do-- we built our own crematorium.
A crematorium meaning just an oven to burn the dead bodies, you know.
I worked there too for awhile.
But it was nothing.
Just take the bodies and put them in and burn them and-- that was it.
But they were not killing.
You know, there was no gas chamber or anything.
That's just for dead people.
Till they built the hospital.
It didn't take long.
They built a hospital.
But this was just in the beginning.
Where somebody was really sick, you know, they were-- and then I was very, very fortunate.
I got into the kitchen, working into the kitchen as a kartoffelschaler.
Kartoffelschaler means peeling potatoes.
Just for the kitchen.
Like prepare the food for the kitchen.
And it was called kartoffelschaler to just peel potatoes all day long.
And it was good.
It was inside, you know, winter time.
It was good.
I was young.
And I kept there for-- I was working there for quite awhile.
And then they built up a hospital.
And for awhile, it was very good.
We thought, well, things are getting brighter for us in Warschau ghetto.
We could-- we didn't have a bad SS group.
It was sort of a medium German soldiers looking after the particular camp.
And the boys, they found a lot of gold.
They used to trade it in, you know.
We used to even had-- like you could buy things.
After all the trouble, it was terrific.
And I used to work in the kitchen, and I used to go twice a week into the Warsaw city with a German and a truck and bring the bread for the prisoners.
And then I used to have gold with me, different things.
So I used to tell the German, can I go to the store?
So the German came with me to the store.
I used to buy cheeses and even vodka.
You know, will let me bring into the camp.
It was OK, you know?
Everybody, all the Greeks there, found a lot of-- a lot of gold diamonds and all that.
So it was changing.
It was like from Birkenau-Auschwitz to this, for the beginning, was very good.
We don't talk to any people in Warsaw.
The partisans were there in Warsaw.
I'll give you an incident of what happened.
One of handsome boy, a Greek handsome-- really handsome man-- he was working in camp, in Warschau camp, changing where the shirts are.
You know, like change them to-- take them to the cleaners every week.
He used to go with a German-- what do you call this-- truck, and take the old clothes, and bring cleaner clothes to the camp for us.
And he used to be in charge of this thing.
So he used to go once a week into this city, into this big laundry thing.
And a Polish girl fall in love with him, a worker.
Not a prisoner, just an ordinary worker in civilian life.
So when he told them that he wants-- he fall in love.
He liked her.
So he says, we'll organize to save you.
The partisans will meet you there.
So they give her when they're coming in-- what do you call?
When the wagon, you'll be coming back to the camp, you know?
The truck with the partisans will be waiting for you there.
You try and run.
And they'll grab you.
They'll put you in the truck and take you to the mountains.
Everything was set, fine.
And it happened.
It did.
And all of a sudden, he ran away.
And one of these soldiers, one of the partisans shot one of the Germans-- it was two Germans with him.
And then, when he started running, the other guy shot him, this Greek guy, in the leg.
He fell down.
He couldn't run into the truck, so the truck left.
And they left him there.
So they brought him back into camp.
And he was such a handsome-- a good, nice looking man.
They hanged him.
You know, after two weeks, or a week.
He recuperate from the wound.
So they hang it in front of us.
That was one of the saddest thing that we could see in Warsaw.
And it was-- it going on there for a while.
And then, all of a sudden, those German groups left a camp there, bunch of son of a bitches.
Real bad SS.
Real-- they came from another camp.
From Buchenwald, I don't know from where.
One of those bad camps.
They took over.
They cut off everything.
They cut what we used to have.
You know, we could buy things, cigarettes and everything else.
Everything was completely out.
And we were ready-- now they start the agony again.
For awhile, for a few months-- I would say we were there almost a year.
For a few months, we had like a paradise.
We could never believe it, but after what we saw in Birkenau and Auschwitz.
But it became like tyranny there.
But I was working in the kitchen.
I wasn't-- I had no fear.
And then all of a sudden, typhus came into the Warsaw ghetto camp, in our camp.
And it was bad.
And I had a good friend of mind, which I survived.
If he's alive today-- which he is.
I have a picture of him.
He's alive today.
He's an economist.
So he had typhus.
And he was in this particular barrack with a sort of a hospital there was.
Zakiniko-- ?] I have the picture here-- [?
Namias. ?] [?
Zakiniko ?] [?
Namias. ?] I have the picture.
Red with freckles on.
My age.
As a matter of fact, he's six months younger than me.
I always trying to save this guy.
Because wherever I used to go, he used to be behind me.
But he was always unlucky.
They used to kick him out.
At least I always managed to work at the kitchen.
It's what we call [INAUDIBLE] in the camps.
There's a story behind him.
So the finale was that I went to the doctors, and the people were looking after the typhus people.
He survived.
They were dying like flies, but he came out.
And it was the happiest day of my life.
Came back to Warsaw to work with me in the kartoffelschaler, with me at the kitchen.
But the things are-- for the rest of the Greeks, not for me.
I mean, I still-- I manage.
I was the same weight.
I was-- and I never got sick.
That's the beauty of my camp, that I was-- because a lot of people got sick.
Some, they made it in the hospital.
Some of them came back.
But so happened, I didn't eat dirt.
You know, people were eating-- used to go to the garbage eating potato shells full of garbage.
They were eating grass, you name it.
Whatever they found, they would eat.
I happened not to eat this, and my stomach shrunk.
I saved myself.
I didn't eat this dirt.
And the people that got sick and died, it was from eating all kinds of dirt they were finding.
And I never ate that stuff, and I never got sick.
Which, thank God for that.
That's because if I would have got sick, go to a hospital, I'd never make it.
Because I was skinny.
I was only 100 and somewhat pounds.
So finally, time goes by.
The same routine in Warsaw, day after day.
We don't know nothing what's happening.
And all of a sudden, we hear rumors that the Russians are only 60 kilometers away from Warsaw.
Well, the Germans are ready.
They start pulling us together.
[INAUDIBLE].
The sick people left them over there.
They shot them in Warschau.
And us, we were good.
So they put us-- we got dressed and started walking.
We walked through Poland three days.

We were walking through the day and staying overnight near the water somewhere where we-- we were about 3,000 people already in Warschau, because [INAUDIBLE] people coming in every so often with new transport coming in.
We had some Hungarian Jews already, and some French Jews come in.
The first tractor was only Greeks.
Then still coming French and Hungarians.
And the camp, we became big.
It was almost 2,500-3,000 people.
And we were marching.
I don't know where we were marching, but we were marching.
We didn't know where we were going.
And whoever stayed in the back, they shot them.
You know, couldn't walk anymore?
They shot them.
They'd dump them in the corners down there.
Did you see any local people during this walk?
No.
No.
No.
Well, maybe the odd farmer.
Because for the streets they were taking us, there was no-- they weren't taking us through major streets.
You know, they were taking through places there were no nobody was there.
But at night, you used to have a rest on the grass, if somewhere was grass.
And we used to sleep.
Sleep without clothes, without anything.
It was hot.
It was summer.
It was around August, I think.
It was really hot.
After walking the-- I think it was about, I don't know, 30 kilometers or something-- they put us into trains.
They give us one can of meat and a half a loaf of bread, or a loaf of bread.
And it was salty.
The meat was salty.
We were dying for water.
Finally, they put us into trains, wagons.
You know, the cattle wagons has those big doors in the middle.
They put us 45, one wagon on this side, and 45 on the other side.
And the big doors was occupied by one German and two Polish couples.
You know, gentiles.
And they put us sitting on the floor, one top of the other.
I think it was five rows of nine on each side of the-- what do you call-- of the wagon.
It was unbearable.
You couldn't move.
So finally, there were people dying.
It was [INAUDIBLE] killing each other.
As soon as we used to pass through-- what do you call-- a river to dump the bodies, you know?
And those Germans, and the kapos, they were hitting us because we were screaming.
We couldn't move.
It was so bad.
And we wanted to-- I don't believe it was very long that we were in the trains.
But from the 90 people that we got into the wagons, only about 60-65 arrived in Dachau.
We arrived in Dachau.
We take at Dachau.
The people who came to pick us up, he takes a look at the shape that we were in.
And some of them, we told them what happened.
They shouldn't have done it.
Dachau wasn't a bad camp.
Dachau was a concentration camp, but it wasn't like Auschwitz or Birkenau or Buchenwald, or Bergen-Belsen, where you heard all those camps.
But Dachau was a good camp.
They took us over there, and they took all the couples.
And they give them to hard labor.
What they did to us.
You know, because they saw, here you're supposed to bring in 3,000 people.
You only got 2,000.
Where are the rest of them?
They dump them under-- through the rivers, as the train was going by.
It wasn't bad, Dachau.
So I don't remember too much of Dachau, because I was only there a very, very short time.
They give me a number-- 87,790, written with a marker.
You know, like sewn into the-- what do you call-- into the jacket.
That was my number in Dachau.
This number on a hand was already obsolete.
They don't-- doesn't count anymore.
So we were there for-- I don't remember.
We weren't there very long.
And-- What were the living conditions like in Dachau.
In Dachau, it wasn't bad.
Because we weren't there very long.
They look after us.
We had something to eat.
You know, and it was clean.
It was only three or four people, and we were sleeping in each bunker, you know.
It wasn't bad at all.
As a matter of fact, I don't have no complaints about Dachau.
After all, what I went through-- when you see something better, you appreciate it, you see.
And we were there for a while, and then they start taking us-- spread around to different camps, the whole group of us.
Some went to Muehldorf.
There was a camp named Muehldorf.
Me, they send me to Waldlager.
Waldlager means 'inside the forest.' This camp was already done.
It was made up-- it was about 20 in a bunker.
It was underground, with wooden boards.
And there was covered, like a peak.
You know?
Like a tent.
Like a tent, but we were underground.
We were sleeping there.
There, I was-- again, I was very fortunate.
As soon as I got down there, they wanted to kartoffelschaler, peeler, you know.
And I got down there, and then they wanted two people to cut sauerkraut.
Sauerkraut is cabbage cut them in two, and make sauerkraut.
Make what do you call-- like how do you say sauerkraut in-- sauerkraut--
Like a coleslaw?
Coleslaw, yeah.
But the Germans are very good for sauerkraut.
The German soldiers, sauerkraut is one of the best meals they can have.
And as a matter of fact, when you go eat meat, they have sauerkraut on top of it.
Anyway, I was taught how to cut the things.
And they were sending me for awhile in the German house, cutting it and making the sauerkraut for the Germans.
And then I was working in the kitchen.
The camp, it wasn't bad.
You know?
It was hard labor.
Very, very hard labor.
They were working baustelle.
A baustelle is making bunkers out of cement, which the aeroplanes-- aeroplane hangars, but made of cement.
And they call it a baustelle.
Well, I was-- I never worked there.
I always work in the kitchen.
And I was good.
But this little, it's a [?
Zakiniko ?] [?
Namias ?] that I was telling you about.
I took him to work with me.
All of a sudden, And two weeks later, they throw him out.
You know?
And all of a sudden, he went to work in the baustelle.
He was a young kid.
And this Leon Yachiel, the guy who was in Birkenau-- you remember, I told you Birkenau, he was the Polish Jew-- you know, from Polish decent?
He became an [INAUDIBLE] an [INAUDIBLE], the big chief [INAUDIBLE].
He had a big position.
And a few of the Greeks became-- Jewish Greeks became kapos.
Which is, you know, normal.
And this particular [?
Zakiniko, ?] he got into-- put him into work in the baustelle.
In the meantime, this Leon Yachiel used to come during the day after everybody went to work, used to come to the kitchen.
I used to prepare different foods for him, make some potatoes into the oven, you know.
And I used to make potato latkes.
You know what a potato latke is?
I used to [INAUDIBLE].
I made potato latkes with the kitchen, you know.
It was pretty good.
Because I slept in the kitchen.
I didn't sleep in the camp.
So that was pretty good.
It was habit.
And all of a sudden, it's like, you know, he got sick.
And he has to go work night shift.
I went to Leon.
I went to the camp.
Leon?
I says, he's sick.
He's a young kid.
He's going to die, you know?
He's got fever.
You're going to send him to the baustelle.
He started screaming at me.
He forgot how much I did for him during the day.
He was nervous.
He says, well, I can't do it.
I can't take him out.
I says, put somebody else.
He's going to die.
You know?
[INAUDIBLE] you know what I did?
I took my eyes to an apron.
I was wet.
Apron when I was working on potatoes.
I took my apron, and I says to Itzak, I says, you go to sleep.
I'll go for you to work at night.
Because nobody knew about me.
You see, I was-- and I went to work for him.
And I didn't want him to go to work at the baustelle, because he was sick.
And all of a sudden, I'm working on the line, and it was cold.
It was winter, freezing.
The Polish, the mustache was all frozen from the cold.
And I was wet.
And I didn't have much clothes on.
So now I'm walking on the line, on foot to the baustelle.
And all of a sudden, one of the Greek kapos saw me.
And I happened to-- every day, I used to steal two big pots of soup.
In the camp, it was Greeks and non-Greeks.
I used to steal two 40-liter soups, put them on the side, and the Greeks used to come to divider for all the Greeks.
I used to do that every night.
And they knew me, you know?
They needed the food.
And the kapo knew me.
This kapo was-- [INAUDIBLE] was his name.
He sees me on the line.
He used to call [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] means I'm blind.
My eyes was a little crooked at those days.
And my nickname, in camp, was [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] means blind.
He says to me, [INAUDIBLE], what are you doing here?
Well, I says-- I tell him the story.
I says, rico.
Rico means the red-haired kid, which is a-- it was sick.
And Leon wouldn't take him off.
And I had to force.
He says, OK.
He says, don't worry about it [INAUDIBLE].
We'll go to the baustelle.
As soon as we were going to the baustelle, he says, here.
I'll give you a stick.
Go to the bathroom where the people go to the washroom to, you know-- there's no washroom, just an open pit, you know.
You [INAUDIBLE] and then I'll come.
But an hour later, I'll come and take you.
I'll pick you up.
In the baustelle was a barrack called Red Cross barrack in case of-- so he comes-- he comes in with two guys.
And he brings me out a white towel.
He wraps it all over my face.
And two guys are carrying me, you know?
And he takes me to the Red Cross.
He says, sleep.
I'll wake you up tomorrow morning and take you back to camp.
And then that's where the hard work that I did.
Because I used to help everybody.
And all the Greeks in Israel and in Greece, wherever they are, they all know me because what I did for them when I had the position, you see?
And like this, this Greek boy survive.
And I was there.
Took us quite a while, and all of a sudden, we hear that the-- what do you call?
We were in Waldlager, which is already in Germany.
And the Russians coming in and bombing the whole place.
You know, we could see at night, they put what do you call, those lights, to make it-- Floodlights.
Floodlights to throw the bombs out.
We could see it was coming to the end.
But still, we have no news.
We have no news, but we can see already there is no Auschwitz and there's no Birkenau, because there's on crematoriums where we are.
We're already in German soil.
But we had no choice.
But still we had to do the work.
You know, going with the guys, my guys used to go to baustelle, go to work.
And as far as food's concerned, we had-- not enough, but what I was used to steal for my group that I had, I was helping them out, which I was very happy.
And I used to have a big stick in my hand, actually scream [INAUDIBLE].
In the meantime, I said to them in Spanish, where the two big parts are for my Greek friends to grab them, you see.
Because I cannot give it to them.
I just put them on the corner.
And I yell, you know, [INAUDIBLE] in Spanish.
And they went to grab the two parts of [INAUDIBLE].
They used to divide it themselves.
Well, it wasn't-- no compare to Auschwitz or Birkenau.
No compare it to the other camps that I had been.
Not too many people died there, even though we were only a short time.
But didn't see no many dead people there.
As long as they were working at the baustelle.
They were going into work-- it was hard work, carrying bags of cement up or down the stairs, you know?
Because I saw where they were working.
I didn't work, but-- But it wasn't extremely dangerous.
We had no fear there was going to be a selection.
That was the biggest fear, you know, that we had.
We never-- the fear you go to the hospital, you don't come out.
There was-- towards the end, they were talking about now end of '44.
You know, beginning of '45, actually.
So while the Russians were advancing, so they decide, well, they've got to evacuate us.
Muehldorf Lager was only about 10 kilometers or 15 kilometers from our Waldlager.
So they took trains, and they put us in big trains.
The both lagers together.
And from what I understood was that they were taking us to the mountains of [INAUDIBLE] mountains, [INAUDIBLE] mountains, which is near Switzerland there somewhere.
And over there, they were going to eliminate us, to kill us.
We'll go to break now.
Break.
